_meta.authoringNotes: Burden, geographic distribution, and elimination targets from WHO Leishmaniasis fact sheet 2024 and WHO Global Leishmaniasis Surveillance 2023 (Weekly Epidemiological Record). Clinical syndromes per Burza et al. Lancet 2018 (PMID 30126638), Murray et al. Lancet 2005 (PMID 16291068), and Aronson et al. CID 2017 IDSA/ASTMH guidelines (PMID 27941151). VL treatment regimens per Sundar et al. NEJM 2010 single-dose liposomal amphotericin B (PMID 20107214) and Pandey et al. Clin Infect Dis 2022. India Kala-Azar Elimination Programme status per NVBDCP/NCVBDC 2024 reports. HIV-VL coinfection per WHO 2022 guidelines and Diro et al. PLoS Negl Trop Dis 2014 (PMID 24809506). PKDL data per Zijlstra et al. Lancet Infect Dis 2017 (PMID 28365228). Cutaneous leishmaniasis treatment per WHO 2010 expert consultation.

h1Title: Leishmaniasis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Leishmaniasis is a parasitic disease caused by more than 20 species of Leishmania protozoa transmitted by infected female phlebotomine sandflies. It presents in three main clinical forms with very different consequences: cutaneous leishmaniasis (skin ulcers, around 600,000-1,000,000 new cases each year), mucocutaneous leishmaniasis (destructive ulceration of the nose, mouth, and throat, mainly in Latin America), and visceral leishmaniasis or kala-azar (a systemic disease with fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and pancytopenia that is fatal within months in over 95% of untreated patients). WHO estimates 50,000-90,000 new visceral cases per year, with more than 90% reported from Brazil, Ethiopia, Eritrea, India, Kenya, Somalia, South Sudan, Sudan, and Yemen. India's Kala-Azar Elimination Programme has cut visceral leishmaniasis incidence by more than 95% since 2007 and reached the WHO elimination-as-public-health-problem target (under 1 case per 10,000 at block level) across most of Bihar. Treatment is now stratified by region and form: a single dose of liposomal amphotericin B cures more than 95% of visceral leishmaniasis in the Indian subcontinent, while combination regimens are used in Africa and HIV coinfection.

keyStats.prevalence: Cutaneous leishmaniasis: approximately 600,000-1,000,000 new cases per year; visceral leishmaniasis: 50,000-90,000 new cases per year (WHO 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Endemic in 90+ countries on five continents; over 1 billion people live in endemic areas. Visceral leishmaniasis concentrated in Brazil, East Africa, and Indian subcontinent
keyStats.avgAge: Visceral leishmaniasis peaks under age 15 in endemic regions; cutaneous leishmaniasis any age
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 12 million people currently infected globally; visceral leishmaniasis carries 95%+ mortality if untreated

definition: Leishmaniasis (ICD-10: B55) is a group of parasitic infections caused by obligate intracellular Leishmania protozoa transmitted to humans through bites of infected female phlebotomine sandflies (Phlebotomus species in the Old World; Lutzomyia in the New World). Promastigotes injected during the sandfly blood meal are taken up by macrophages, transform into amastigotes, and replicate inside macrophage phagolysosomes. The clinical syndrome depends on the infecting species, the host immune response, and the geographic setting. Cutaneous leishmaniasis is caused by Leishmania major and Leishmania tropica in the Old World and Leishmania mexicana, Leishmania amazonensis, and Leishmania braziliensis complex in the New World, producing localized, diffuse, or disseminated skin lesions. Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis (espundia) is caused mainly by Leishmania braziliensis in Bolivia, Peru, and Brazil and destroys oronasopharyngeal mucosa months to years after the initial cutaneous infection. Visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar) is caused by Leishmania donovani in the Indian subcontinent and East Africa and Leishmania infantum (also known as Leishmania chagasi) around the Mediterranean and in Latin America; parasites disseminate to reticuloendothelial organs, producing fever, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and hypergammaglobulinemia. Post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis (PKDL) is a hypopigmented, papular, or nodular skin eruption that follows apparent cure of visceral leishmaniasis in 5-10% of Indian patients and up to 50% of Sudanese patients, serving as a major reservoir for ongoing transmission. The disease is managed by tropical medicine, infectious disease, dermatology, and pediatrics teams, with public-health elimination programmes coordinated by WHO and ministries of health.

typesClassification.description: Most common form, with around 600,000-1,000,000 new cases per year. Causes one or more skin ulcers at the site of sandfly bites — exposed face, arms, and lower legs — beginning as a papule that enlarges into a painless 1-5 cm ulcer with raised borders. Heals over months to years leaving a depressed scar.

Destructive ulceration of the mucous membranes of the nose, mouth, palate, and pharynx that appears months to years after an apparently healed cutaneous lesion. Mainly caused by Leishmania braziliensis in Bolivia, Peru, and Brazil. Can cause severe disfigurement and airway compromise.

Systemic infection of reticuloendothelial organs (spleen, liver, bone marrow). Fever, weight loss, massive hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, and hypergammaglobulinemia. Fatal in over 95% of untreated patients within 2 years of symptom onset.

Hypopigmented macules and papules that evolve into nodules, especially on the face, arms, and trunk, appearing months to years after VL treatment. Occurs in roughly 5-10% of Indian and up to 50% of Sudanese VL patients. PKDL patients are a key reservoir for ongoing transmission.

Rare anergic form resembling lepromatous leprosy: multiple non-ulcerating papules and nodules covering large areas of the body in patients with poor cell-mediated immunity. Difficult to treat and often relapses.
Visceral leishmaniasis in HIV-infected individuals presents atypically, responds less well to standard therapy, and relapses frequently. Co-endemic in Ethiopia, Brazil, and the Mediterranean basin.

primarySymptoms: A painless papule at the site of a sandfly bite (exposed face, arms, or lower legs) that enlarges over weeks into a 1-5 cm ulcer with raised, indurated borders and a clean central crater — the classic cutaneous leishmaniasis lesion.
Multiple cutaneous ulcers, satellite nodules along lymphatic drainage, or a single non-healing skin ulcer over six weeks in someone with travel to or residence in an endemic region.
Persistent, low-grade fever (often double-quotidian — twice daily) with progressive weight loss over weeks to months — a hallmark of visceral leishmaniasis.
Progressive, often massive enlargement of the spleen (commonly palpable below the umbilicus) and moderate hepatomegaly in visceral leishmaniasis.
Pallor, fatigue, dyspnoea on exertion, easy bruising, and recurrent infections from pancytopenia (anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia) in advanced visceral leishmaniasis.
Skin darkening (kala-azar is Hindi for 'black sickness') on hands, feet, abdomen, and face in chronic visceral leishmaniasis.
Hypopigmented macules evolving into papules and nodules, especially on the face, arms, and trunk, appearing months to years after VL treatment — post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis.
Progressive nasal congestion, epistaxis, ulceration of the nasal septum, and palatal perforation in mucocutaneous leishmaniasis.
Hoarseness, dysphagia, and progressive disfigurement when mucocutaneous disease involves the pharynx and larynx.
Bleeding gums, recurrent fevers, and opportunistic infections in HIV-VL coinfection.
earlyWarningSigns: A non-healing painless skin ulcer or papule for more than 4-6 weeks at a site previously exposed to insect bites in an endemic region
Persistent low-grade fever with palpable spleen in a child or young adult from rural Bihar, eastern Sudan, or rural Brazil
Unexplained progressive splenomegaly in a person with no obvious haematological or hepatic explanation
Recurrent epistaxis, nasal crusting, or ulceration in a person who travelled to or lived in the Amazon basin years earlier
Hypopigmented macules and papules on the face of a person treated for kala-azar within the past 1-2 years
emergencySigns: Severe pancytopenia with bleeding or systemic infection in visceral leishmaniasis — requires admission for transfusion, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent leishmaniasis treatment
Hemodynamic instability or splenic rupture from massive splenomegaly
Airway compromise from mucocutaneous leishmaniasis affecting larynx or pharynx
Severe drug reactions to pentavalent antimonials — QT prolongation, arrhythmia, pancreatitis, hepatitis — particularly in older or HIV-coinfected patients
VL relapse in HIV coinfection with severe sepsis or multi-organ failure

causes.cause: Infection by Leishmania donovani complex causing visceral disease
causes.description: Leishmania donovani drives anthroponotic VL in the Indian subcontinent (India, Nepal, Bangladesh) and East Africa (Sudan, South Sudan, Ethiopia). Leishmania infantum (synonym L. chagasi) drives zoonotic VL around the Mediterranean basin and in Latin America, with domestic dogs as the principal reservoir. The parasite is transmitted by Phlebotomus argentipes in India, P. orientalis in East Africa, and P. perniciosus in the Mediterranean.

causes.cause: Infection by Old World cutaneous Leishmania species
causes.description: Leishmania major causes zoonotic CL in rural Middle East, North Africa, and Central Asia with rodent reservoirs and Phlebotomus papatasi vectors. Leishmania tropica causes anthroponotic CL in urban Middle Eastern, Mediterranean, and Asian foci with Phlebotomus sergenti vectors. Leishmania aethiopica causes CL and diffuse CL in Ethiopian highlands.

causes.cause: Infection by New World cutaneous and mucocutaneous Leishmania species
causes.description: Leishmania mexicana complex causes CL in Mexico, Central America, and parts of South America. Leishmania braziliensis complex (L. braziliensis, L. panamensis, L. guyanensis) cause CL that can progress to mucocutaneous disease in Bolivia, Peru, Ecuador, and Brazil. Lutzomyia species are vectors. Forest mammals are reservoirs.

Female sandflies smaller than 3 mm bite at dusk and dawn and through the night. They breed in cracks in dwellings, animal sheds, rodent burrows, and forest leaf litter. A single infected sandfly bite can transmit the parasite; bites are often unnoticed because of the small mouthparts.

causes.cause: Person-to-person transmission via blood and shared injecting equipment
causes.description: Anthroponotic VL caused by L. donovani persists through human-to-sandfly-to-human transmission; PKDL patients are an under-recognized reservoir. Transmission via blood transfusion, organ transplantation, congenital infection, and shared needles in injecting drug users has been documented.

HIV coinfection, solid-organ transplantation, and TNF-alpha inhibitor therapy can reactivate latent leishmaniasis and produce severe, relapsing disease. HIV-VL coinfection accounts for a substantial proportion of VL cases in Ethiopia and is rising in Brazil.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in an endemic region
riskFactors.description: Endemic in 90+ countries. Bihar (India), Terai (Nepal), eastern Sudan, South Sudan, Ethiopia, north-east Brazil, Mediterranean basin, and rural Iran, Iraq, Afghanistan, Syria are major foci. Sandflies are most active at dusk and dawn and feed indoors and outdoors.
Living in mud or thatch-walled houses, sleeping outdoors, or working in agriculture, forestry, or military operations in endemic areas increases exposure to sandfly bites.
Visceral leishmaniasis is strongly associated with poverty, malnutrition, and poor housing. Income gradient is steep in Bihar and East Africa.
Children with protein-calorie malnutrition are at higher risk of progressing from asymptomatic infection to symptomatic VL. Vitamin A and zinc deficiency further increase risk.

HIV-VL coinfection produces atypical presentations, poorer treatment response, and higher relapse rates. Solid-organ transplant recipients and patients on TNF inhibitors can reactivate latent infection.

riskFactors.factor: Domestic dog ownership in zoonotic transmission zones
riskFactors.description: In Mediterranean and Latin American VL foci, domestic dogs are the principal reservoir for Leishmania infantum. Canine vaccines and insecticidal collars reduce human risk.

riskFactors.factor: Recent prior cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania braziliensis complex
riskFactors.description: Roughly 1-10% of L. braziliensis cutaneous infections progress to mucocutaneous disease months to years later; risk persists for decades.

affectedDemographics: Indian subcontinent: historically dominant focus of L. donovani visceral leishmaniasis. The Kala-Azar Elimination Programme has cut incidence by >95% since 2007 and most endemic blocks of Bihar are now below the WHO elimination threshold.
East Africa: Sudan, South Sudan, Ethiopia, Eritrea, Kenya, Somalia bear the heaviest VL burden, with severe outbreaks driven by conflict, malnutrition, and HIV coinfection.
Latin America: Brazil has the largest L. infantum visceral burden in the Western Hemisphere; cutaneous and mucocutaneous disease across the Amazon basin.
Mediterranean basin: Spain, Italy, Greece, Turkey have ongoing L. infantum VL, mainly in immunocompromised hosts and children.
Middle East: explosive cutaneous leishmaniasis outbreaks have followed conflict and population displacement in Syria, Iraq, Afghanistan, and Yemen.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis depends on the clinical form. For cutaneous leishmaniasis, parasitological confirmation by smear, biopsy, or culture from the active edge of the lesion is standard; Giemsa-stained tissue smears show amastigotes inside macrophages, and PCR is more sensitive especially for old lesions. Speciation by PCR or isoenzyme analysis matters because treatment differs between species (Leishmania braziliensis complex requires systemic therapy because of mucocutaneous risk; Leishmania major may resolve spontaneously). For mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, biopsy with histology, smear, and PCR is needed and serology supports the diagnosis. For visceral leishmaniasis, the rK39 immunochromatographic rapid test is the field-standard screening tool: sensitivity over 95% and specificity over 90% on Indian subcontinent samples, slightly lower in East Africa where rK28 or rK39 plus direct agglutination test are used in tandem. Splenic aspirate is the most sensitive parasitological test (sensitivity >95%) but carries bleeding risk and is reserved for treatment-failure or relapse evaluation; bone marrow aspirate (sensitivity 60-85%) is safer and often used at presentation. Liver aspirate and lymph-node aspiration are alternatives. Quantitative PCR on peripheral blood is increasingly used to confirm diagnosis, monitor treatment response, and detect relapse in HIV-VL coinfection. Differential diagnosis for visceral leishmaniasis includes lymphoma, leukemia, miliary tuberculosis, malaria, chronic schistosomiasis, hyper-reactive malarial splenomegaly, and tropical splenomegaly syndrome. For cutaneous lesions, the differential includes squamous cell carcinoma, atypical mycobacterial infection, deep fungal infection, syphilis, leprosy, and pyoderma gangrenosum.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid bedside screening for visceral leishmaniasis with very high sensitivity in the Indian subcontinent
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A drop of finger-prick blood applied to a lateral-flow cassette; result in 10-20 minutes. Sensitivity >95% in India and Nepal, around 80% in East Africa. Antibody can remain positive after cure, so does not separate active from past disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow aspirate with Giemsa stain and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Parasitological confirmation of visceral leishmaniasis with moderate sensitivity (60-85%) and excellent specificity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure with local anesthesia at the posterior iliac crest; mild discomfort during marrow aspiration. Result within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highest sensitivity parasitological test for VL (>95%); reserved for treatment-failure or relapse where bone marrow and rK39 are non-diagnostic
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief outpatient procedure under aseptic conditions; needle inserted into the enlarged spleen with bedside cardiovascular monitoring. Risk of bleeding 0.1-0.5%; contraindicated with severe thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin lesion smear or punch biopsy with Giemsa stain, culture, and PCR (CL/MCL)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms cutaneous or mucocutaneous leishmaniasis and identifies species
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small scraping or 4 mm punch biopsy from the lesion edge under local anesthesia. Smear result same day; PCR and culture within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Leishmania PCR (qPCR or kinetoplast DNA assays)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Sensitive confirmation in tissue, blood, and bone marrow; quantitates parasite load for treatment monitoring; identifies species
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Tissue or blood sample sent to a reference laboratory; results within 3-7 days. Useful in HIV-VL coinfection and PKDL.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, biochemistry, and protein electrophoresis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects pancytopenia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and impaired hepatic and renal function characteristic of VL
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 24 hours. Hypoalbuminemia with markedly elevated globulin (reversed A:G ratio) is characteristic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coinfection that affects treatment choice, prognosis, and follow-up duration
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard rapid test or laboratory ELISA; result in 30 minutes to 24 hours. Recommended for every VL patient in endemic regions.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is stratified by clinical form, infecting species, geographic region, and immune status. For visceral leishmaniasis on the Indian subcontinent, WHO recommends single-dose intravenous liposomal amphotericin B 10 mg/kg, which cures more than 95% of patients with low toxicity. Where liposomal amphotericin B is unavailable, multidose regimens (15 mg/kg total over several days), miltefosine (50-100 mg orally daily for 28 days), or combinations are used. In East Africa, mortality is higher and combination therapy with sodium stibogluconate plus paromomycin for 17 days is standard, with new combinations of liposomal amphotericin B and miltefosine showing promise. For HIV-VL coinfection, induction with high-dose liposomal amphotericin B followed by secondary prophylaxis until immune reconstitution is required because relapse is common. For cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by species at risk of mucocutaneous progression (Leishmania braziliensis complex), systemic pentavalent antimonials (sodium stibogluconate or meglumine antimoniate 20 mg/kg/day for 20 days), liposomal amphotericin B, or miltefosine are used. For uncomplicated Old World cutaneous leishmaniasis with single lesions, local therapy may suffice: intralesional pentavalent antimonials, cryotherapy, thermotherapy, paromomycin ointment, or fluconazole. For mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, prolonged systemic pentavalent antimony, liposomal amphotericin B, or miltefosine is required, and follow-up extends years because of risk of relapse. Post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis is treated with extended miltefosine, liposomal amphotericin B, or paromomycin combinations to reduce ongoing transmission. Adjunctive supportive care includes blood transfusion for severe anemia, antibiotics for secondary bacterial infections, nutritional rehabilitation, and HIV treatment in coinfection. Public-health control includes vector control with insecticidal nets and indoor residual spraying, surveillance, prompt treatment of cases, dog reservoir control where applicable, and PKDL detection and treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B 10 mg/kg single intravenous dose (Indian subcontinent VL)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard of care for VL in the Indian subcontinent. Single dose delivered over 2 hours. Common acute reactions are fever, chills, and back pain; most are mild and managed with premedication.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate >95% in Indian VL (Sundar NEJM 2010, PMID 20107214); lower in East Africa where higher cumulative doses (15-20 mg/kg) are used

medicalTreatments.name: Miltefosine 50-100 mg orally daily for 28 days
medicalTreatments.description: First oral drug for leishmaniasis. Used for VL, CL, MCL, and PKDL. Contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity; contraceptive cover required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate around 90% in Indian VL; lower in East Africa; effective oral option for CL and MCL caused by selected species

medicalTreatments.name: Pentavalent antimonials (sodium stibogluconate / meglumine antimoniate) 20 mg Sb⁵⁺/kg/day for 20-28 days
medicalTreatments.description: Historical mainstay still used widely in Africa, Latin America, and resource-limited settings. Cardiotoxicity (QT prolongation, T-wave changes), pancreatitis, and hepatitis require monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 70-95% depending on region and species; failure widespread in Indian L. donovani — no longer used there

medicalTreatments.name: Paromomycin (aminosidine) 15 mg/kg/day intramuscular for 21 days
medicalTreatments.description: Aminoglycoside with activity against Leishmania. Used in combination regimens for VL in Africa and as topical 15% paromomycin/methylbenzethonium chloride or paromomycin/gentamicin for CL.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate around 80-90% in African VL when combined with sodium stibogluconate; topical paromomycin effective for Old World CL

medicalTreatments.name: Combination therapy (liposomal amphotericin B + miltefosine; sodium stibogluconate + paromomycin)
medicalTreatments.description: Shorter course, improved efficacy, reduced relapse, and reduced drug pressure in regions where monotherapy efficacy is declining or where HIV coinfection is common.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 95%+ in Indian VL (single-dose LAMB + miltefosine); ~90% in East African VL with SSG+paromomycin combination

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional pentavalent antimonials (sodium stibogluconate)
medicalTreatments.description: Local injection into cutaneous leishmaniasis lesions at 2 mL per lesion every week for 3-8 sessions. Useful for small, accessible Old World CL lesions without mucocutaneous risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 70-90% for Old World CL; sparing systemic toxicity

medicalTreatments.name: Cryotherapy and thermotherapy for cutaneous lesions
medicalTreatments.description: Liquid-nitrogen cryotherapy or controlled localized heat (50 °C for 30 seconds) destroys parasites within the lesion. Useful for L. tropica and L. major lesions, often combined with intralesional antimonials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 60-90% with combined cryotherapy plus intralesional antimony; well-tolerated outpatient procedure

medicalTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy plus secondary prophylaxis (HIV-VL coinfection)
medicalTreatments.description: Initiation of ART improves cellular immunity and reduces VL relapse. Secondary prophylaxis with liposomal amphotericin B or pentamidine is continued until sustained CD4 recovery above 200 cells/µL for 6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: VL relapse rate falls from 60-90% to under 30% with ART plus secondary prophylaxis (WHO 2022 guidance)

surgicalOptions.name: Splenectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for selected VL patients with massive hypersplenism, hemolysis, or treatment-refractory disease. Risk of severe sepsis from encapsulated organisms requires vaccination and antibiotic prophylaxis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of hypersplenism and pancytopenia in most operated patients; reserved for treatment-failure cases

surgicalOptions.name: Reconstructive surgery for mucocutaneous sequelae
surgicalOptions.description: Plastic and reconstructive surgery after successful antiparasitic therapy can restore nasal contour, palate, and lip in patients with disfiguring mucocutaneous leishmaniasis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable; staged reconstruction often required over 1-3 years

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical paromomycin ointment (15% paromomycin/methylbenzethonium chloride or paromomycin/gentamicin)
alternativeTreatments.description: Effective topical therapy for Old World cutaneous leishmaniasis. Applied twice daily for 20 days. Suited to single small lesions in low-risk species.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oral fluconazole / ketoconazole for selected Old World cutaneous leishmaniasis
alternativeTreatments.description: Fluconazole 200 mg orally daily for 6 weeks improves cure rates for Leishmania major in Saudi Arabia and Iran in randomized trials; ketoconazole has activity against Leishmania mexicana.

whySeeSpecialist: Tropical medicine and infectious disease specialists confirm species, choose region-appropriate drugs, manage drug toxicities (especially with antimonials), and coordinate HIV testing and treatment. Dermatology referral is essential for cutaneous and mucocutaneous disease, and reconstructive surgery follows treatment for severe mucocutaneous sequelae.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician working at or trained by a WHO-collaborating leishmaniasis centre, a national reference laboratory, or a major tropical medicine institute (e.g., Rajendra Memorial Research Institute Patna, Kalaazar Medical Research Center Muzaffarpur, KEMRI Kenya, Médecins Sans Frontières kala-azar programmes). Avoid unverified clinics offering unproven herbal therapies for kala-azar.
hospitalCriteria: Access to rK39 rapid testing and PCR for VL
Pharmacy stocking liposomal amphotericin B and miltefosine
Capacity for bone marrow and (where indicated) splenic aspirate
Cardiology and biochemistry monitoring for patients on antimonials
HIV testing and antiretroviral therapy services
keyFacilities: KEMRI / KAMRC East African leishmaniasis sites
Tertiary tropical medicine and infectious disease units
preventionStrategies: Sleep under a long-lasting insecticidal net (treated with deltamethrin or permethrin) in endemic regions, particularly in Bihar, Nepal, and East Africa.
Use indoor residual spraying programmes targeting sandflies — a cornerstone of the Kala-Azar Elimination Programme.
Apply DEET-, picaridin-, or IR3535-based repellent during dusk and dawn outdoor activity in endemic areas.
Wear long sleeves and trousers during dusk and dawn; sandflies are crepuscular and small enough to pass through normal mesh.
Treat domestic dogs with insecticidal collars or topical preparations in zoonotic L. infantum foci (Mediterranean, Latin America).
Engage with active case detection campaigns; treat PKDL promptly to break the human-to-sandfly transmission cycle.
Vaccinate dogs against canine leishmaniasis where licensed canine vaccines are available (CaniLeish, Letifend).
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full course of treatment as prescribed; partial treatment promotes drug resistance and PKDL.
Attend monthly follow-up after VL treatment for the first 6 months and at 12 months to detect relapse.
Report any new hypopigmented patches or papules after kala-azar treatment promptly.
Avoid sleeping outdoors in endemic regions during peak sandfly activity.
Maintain adequate nutrition during recovery — malnutrition predicts poor outcomes.
Disclose travel history to endemic regions to all clinicians during any future febrile illness, even years later.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) during recovery to address weight loss
Iron-, folate-, and B12-rich foods to support hematologic recovery
Vitamin A- and zinc-rich foods (carrots, leafy greens, oysters) to strengthen mucosal and cellular immunity
Calorie-dense small frequent meals during convalescence
avoid: Alcohol during and shortly after antimonial therapy because of hepatotoxicity
Excess salt during fluid retention with amphotericin therapy
Raw or undercooked meat in immunocompromised patients during treatment
Unverified herbal remedies that may delay effective therapy

exerciseGuidelines: Bed rest during acute febrile phase. Gradual return to activity during convalescence — typically by 4-6 weeks for cutaneous disease and 8-12 weeks for visceral disease. Avoid strenuous exercise during severe pancytopenia and splenomegaly.
dailyManagement: Take oral medications (miltefosine) on a full stomach and at consistent times.
Monitor for nausea, vomiting, or rash and report promptly to prescriber.
Use barrier contraception during and for 4 months after miltefosine therapy in women of reproductive age.
Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen to healing cutaneous lesions to reduce hyperpigmentation.
Attend follow-up at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after VL treatment.
Use insecticide-treated nets at home to prevent re-infection.
prognosis: Outcome is determined chiefly by clinical form, immune status, and prompt access to therapy. Cutaneous leishmaniasis usually heals over months to years; effective treatment shortens healing and reduces scarring. Single Old World lesions caused by L. major may resolve without treatment in 6-18 months but with disfiguring scars. Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis carries a more serious prognosis: prolonged systemic therapy is required, mucosal destruction can recur years after apparent cure, and quality-of-life impact is severe. Visceral leishmaniasis is fatal in over 95% of untreated patients within months; with effective treatment, cure rates exceed 95% on the Indian subcontinent and 80-95% in East Africa, depending on drug regimen. HIV-VL coinfection carries much higher mortality (10-25%) and relapse risk (over 60% without secondary prophylaxis), but ART plus secondary prophylaxis dramatically improves outcomes. PKDL responds to extended therapy but treatment failure and chronic course are common, especially in Sudan. Survivors of VL have generally good long-term function but may have residual splenomegaly for months and require nutritional rehabilitation. The 2030 WHO road map targets verification of elimination as a public-health problem of VL on the Indian subcontinent and 85% of countries achieving sustained control.
recoveryTimeline: Cutaneous leishmaniasis: lesion healing over 1-6 months with active treatment; scars persist. Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis: mucosal healing over 3-12 months; structural recovery often needs years and reconstructive surgery. Visceral leishmaniasis: fever resolves within 7-14 days of treatment; splenomegaly regresses over 1-3 months; blood counts and hypergammaglobulinemia normalize over 2-6 months. PKDL: lesions improve over 12-24 weeks with extended therapy.
complications: Death from untreated visceral leishmaniasis — over 95% mortality within 2 years.
Severe pancytopenia with bleeding, sepsis, and transfusion dependency.
Splenic infarction or rupture from massive splenomegaly.
Mucocutaneous disfigurement — palatal perforation, nasal collapse, laryngeal scarring.
PKDL with chronic depigmented or nodular skin lesions that maintain transmission.
Drug toxicity — pentavalent antimonial-induced QT prolongation, pancreatitis, hepatitis; nephrotoxicity from older amphotericin formulations.
Frequent VL relapse in HIV coinfection without sustained immune reconstitution.
Secondary bacterial infections during severe pancytopenia.

supportResources.name: WHO Leishmaniasis Programme
supportResources.description: Authoritative global epidemiology, treatment guidelines, and elimination targets.

supportResources.name: Drugs for Neglected Diseases initiative (DNDi) — leishmaniasis
supportResources.description: Research and development hub for new and better treatments for VL, MCL, and CL.

supportResources.name: Government of India — National Centre for Vector Borne Diseases Control: Kala-Azar Elimination Programme
supportResources.description: Indian national strategy, district-level data, and treatment protocols.

supportResources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention — Parasites: Leishmaniasis
supportResources.description: Clinical and traveller-focused resource for leishmaniasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause fever and splenomegaly. Malaria has parasites visible on a blood film within hours and responds to artemisinin therapy. Visceral leishmaniasis develops over weeks-months with massive splenomegaly, hypergammaglobulinemia, pancytopenia, and positive rK39 testing. Coinfection is common in endemic regions and both should be tested.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both produce chronic hepatosplenomegaly in tropical regions. Schistosomiasis (S. mansoni) causes periportal fibrosis with preserved hepatocellular function and is diagnosed by stool egg microscopy or serology; treated with praziquantel. Visceral leishmaniasis adds pancytopenia, fever, hypergammaglobulinemia, and amastigotes in bone marrow or spleen.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Miliary TB and TB lymphadenitis can mimic VL with fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, and pancytopenia. Acid-fast bacilli on smear or culture and tuberculin/IGRA tests support TB. Empirical TB treatment without parasitological evaluation can be fatal in unrecognized VL.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus can produce splenomegaly, cytopenias, and hypergammaglobulinemia. Antinuclear antibodies, serositis, malar rash, and other systemic features distinguish it from VL. Skin biopsy and serology differentiate cutaneous lupus from cutaneous leishmaniasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HIV-VL coinfection is increasingly common. HIV alone can cause B-symptoms, lymphadenopathy, and weight loss without the massive splenomegaly typical of VL. All VL patients should be tested for HIV, and all HIV patients with unexplained pancytopenia or splenomegaly from endemic regions should be tested for VL.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic in many leishmaniasis foci; commonly screened together in febrile travellers and residents.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another neglected tropical disease causing hepatosplenomegaly; shared NTD programme infrastructure.

relatedConditions.name: Filariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another vector-borne neglected tropical disease; co-endemic in parts of India and Africa.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Major driver of VL morbidity and mortality in Ethiopia, Brazil, and Mediterranean; coinfection complicates both diseases.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential and frequent coinfection in HIV-positive patients with VL.

faqs.question: What is leishmaniasis?
faqs.answer: Leishmaniasis is a parasitic disease caused by Leishmania protozoa transmitted by sandfly bites. It has three main forms: cutaneous (skin ulcers), mucocutaneous (destructive nose and mouth lesions), and visceral (kala-azar — fatal systemic disease). Endemic across 90+ countries, mainly in tropical and subtropical regions.

faqs.question: What is kala-azar?
faqs.answer: Kala-azar is the Hindi term ('black sickness') for visceral leishmaniasis, the systemic form caused by Leishmania donovani in India and Africa and Leishmania infantum in the Mediterranean and Latin America. It causes fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, and pancytopenia. Untreated, mortality exceeds 95% within two years.

faqs.question: How is leishmaniasis spread?
faqs.answer: Leishmaniasis is transmitted by bites of infected female phlebotomine sandflies, mostly at dusk and dawn. Sandflies pick up parasites from infected humans (anthroponotic transmission) or animal reservoirs such as dogs, rodents, or forest mammals (zoonotic transmission). Person-to-person spread by blood, organ transplant, congenital, and shared needles has also been documented.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of leishmaniasis?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous leishmaniasis starts as a painless papule at a bite site that enlarges into an ulcer over weeks. Visceral leishmaniasis begins with low-grade fever, weight loss, and progressive abdominal swelling from splenomegaly over 2-8 weeks. Mucocutaneous disease can appear months to years after an apparently healed skin lesion as nasal congestion and ulceration.

faqs.question: Is leishmaniasis curable?
faqs.answer: Yes, with appropriate treatment. Single-dose liposomal amphotericin B cures over 95% of visceral leishmaniasis in the Indian subcontinent. Cutaneous disease typically responds to topical, intralesional, or systemic therapy. Mucocutaneous and HIV-coinfected disease require prolonged and combination therapy. Untreated visceral disease is almost always fatal.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for kala-azar?
faqs.answer: In the Indian subcontinent, WHO recommends single-dose intravenous liposomal amphotericin B 10 mg/kg, with cure rates above 95%. Where liposomal amphotericin B is unavailable, multidose regimens, oral miltefosine for 28 days, or combination therapy are used. In East Africa, sodium stibogluconate plus paromomycin for 17 days remains standard.

faqs.question: What is the rK39 test?
faqs.answer: The rK39 immunochromatographic rapid test is a finger-prick antibody test for visceral leishmaniasis. Sensitivity exceeds 95% in the Indian subcontinent and is around 80% in East Africa. Results in 10-20 minutes. Antibody persists after cure, so a positive test in someone previously treated does not by itself prove active disease.

faqs.question: What is post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis?
faqs.answer: Post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis (PKDL) is a hypopigmented, papular, or nodular skin eruption that appears months to years after apparent cure of visceral leishmaniasis. It affects 5-10% of treated Indian patients and up to 50% of Sudanese patients. PKDL is highly infectious to sandflies and is a key reservoir for transmission; prompt treatment is essential.

faqs.question: Can leishmaniasis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse after treatment is uncommon in immunocompetent patients with effective initial therapy (under 5% in Indian VL). Relapse rates are much higher in HIV-VL coinfection (over 60% without secondary prophylaxis). Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis can recur years after apparent cure and requires long follow-up.

faqs.question: How is cutaneous leishmaniasis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on species, lesion size and number, and risk of mucocutaneous progression. Options include topical paromomycin, intralesional pentavalent antimony, cryotherapy, thermotherapy, oral miltefosine or fluconazole, or systemic pentavalent antimony or liposomal amphotericin B. Species at risk of mucocutaneous progression (L. braziliensis complex) require systemic therapy.

faqs.question: Is leishmaniasis common in India?
faqs.answer: Yes — historically India bore the largest VL burden globally, concentrated in Bihar. The Kala-Azar Elimination Programme has cut incidence by more than 95% since 2007, and most endemic blocks now meet the WHO elimination threshold of under 1 case per 10,000 people. Cutaneous leishmaniasis is rare in India.

faqs.question: Can leishmaniasis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, through a combination of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, insect repellents, long clothing at dusk and dawn, and treatment of human and (where applicable) canine reservoirs. The Indian Kala-Azar Elimination Programme combines surveillance, prompt diagnosis, drug treatment, and vector control.

faqs.question: How long is the incubation period of leishmaniasis?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous lesions usually appear 2 weeks to 3 months after the sandfly bite. Visceral leishmaniasis has a longer and more variable incubation period of 2-6 months, sometimes years. Mucocutaneous disease can appear months to many years after an initial cutaneous lesion.

faqs.question: Can leishmaniasis affect HIV-positive people more severely?
faqs.answer: Yes. HIV-VL coinfection produces atypical presentations, blunted antibody response (lower rK39 sensitivity), poorer treatment response, and high relapse rates above 60% without secondary prophylaxis. WHO recommends combination antiparasitic therapy plus antiretroviral therapy and ongoing secondary prophylaxis until sustained CD4 recovery.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for leishmaniasis?
faqs.answer: No licensed human vaccine is currently available, although several candidates are in clinical trials. Canine vaccines (CaniLeish, Letifend) are licensed in Europe and Brazil and reduce risk of zoonotic transmission. Live-attenuated leishmanization (deliberate inoculation with L. major to prevent disfiguring lesions) was historically used in the Middle East.

faqs.question: Why does kala-azar darken the skin?
faqs.answer: Chronic visceral leishmaniasis is associated with hyperpigmentation of the skin of the hands, feet, abdomen, and face — hence the Hindi name kala-azar ('black sickness'). The mechanism involves immune-driven melanocyte activation; pigmentation usually fades over months to years after successful treatment.

faqs.question: What is miltefosine and is it safe?
faqs.answer: Miltefosine is the first oral drug for leishmaniasis, taken at 50-100 mg daily for 28 days. It is effective for VL, CL, MCL, and PKDL. Common side-effects are gastrointestinal upset and transient elevations in transaminases and creatinine. Miltefosine is teratogenic and requires effective contraception during and for 4 months after therapy in women of reproductive age.

faqs.question: Can sandflies transmit other diseases?
faqs.answer: Yes. Phlebotomine sandflies can also transmit sandfly fever viruses (including Sicilian, Naples, and Toscana viruses), bartonellosis (Carrión's disease), and Vesiculovirus species. Vector-control measures for leishmaniasis incidentally reduce risk of these other infections.

faqs.question: Is leishmaniasis contagious between people?
faqs.answer: Leishmaniasis is not typically contagious by casual contact. Anthroponotic VL caused by L. donovani requires the sandfly intermediate vector. However, transmission by blood transfusion, organ transplant, shared needles, and rarely from mother to child has been documented; sexual transmission has been reported anecdotally.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after kala-azar treatment?
faqs.answer: Patients are reviewed at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after treatment. Clinical assessment of spleen size, weight, and energy is combined with full blood count. Active surveillance for PKDL skin lesions continues for at least 12 months. Patients with HIV coinfection require ongoing secondary prophylaxis until sustained CD4 recovery above 200 cells/µL for 6 months.

faqs.question: Can children get kala-azar?
faqs.answer: Yes — children under 15 bear the heaviest VL burden in endemic regions. Pediatric VL responds to weight-based dosing of liposomal amphotericin B. Nutritional rehabilitation, anemia treatment, and treatment of coexistent infections (especially malaria and pneumonia) are essential.

metaDescription: Leishmaniasis and kala-azar: sandfly-borne parasite causing skin, mucosal, and visceral disease. Diagnosis, liposomal amphotericin B, miltefosine reviewed.
